evals.prompt:
Here's our weekly campaign numbers for the Q1 ABM push — Week 4 of 8. Target is 35 SQLs by end of quarter (so ~4.4/week run rate). We're behind.

LinkedIn Sponsored:
  Impressions: 45,200
  Clicks: 158
  CTR: 0.35%
  Spend: $3,740
  Leads (form fills): 6
  Cost per lead: $623

Email nurture (3-email sequence to 1,200 contacts):
  Email 1 — Open: 38%, Click: 5.2%
  Email 2 — Open: 31%, Click: 2.1%
  Email 3 — Open: 22%, Click: 1.4%

Landing page:
  Visits: 491
  Conversions (demo requests): 34
  Conversion rate: 6.9%

Pipeline:
  MQLs this week: 22
  MQL to SAL: 41%
  SAL to Opp: 52%
  SQLs created: 5

Webinar (Tuesday, 68 registrants, 41 attended):
  Attendee to MQL: 29%
  MQL to opp: 8%

Budget: $14,200 spent of $28,000 total (50.7% at 50% of campaign duration — on pace).

Can you run the full performance analysis on this?
evals.expected_output: A structured performance analysis report following the 4-layer framework with headline numbers compared to targets/benchmarks, identification of LinkedIn CTR as at the low end of average (0.35% vs 0.35-0.45% average benchmark), email sequence decay diagnosed with Email 3 as the specific weak point (1.4% CTR = poor tier vs 3.5-5% average), landing page conversion flagged as below average (6.9% vs 10-20% average), webinar-to-opp conversion identified as poor (8% vs 15-20% average), specific actionable recommendations for each underperforming metric with owners and deadlines, recognition of Email 1 performance as above-average (5.2% CTR, above the 3.5-5% average range but below 6% good tier) and MQL-to-SAL as on track (41% = average tier per 35-50% benchmark), and a next-week action plan with assigned owners.
expectations: To tell the CMO where next quarter's budget should shift, LinkedIn CTR (0.35%) is benchmarked against the 0.35-0.45% average tier and classified as bottom-of-average requiring creative improvement, while cost per lead ($623) is flagged as unsustainable for the pipeline math — at 5 SQLs/week the campaign misses the 35-SQL target by end of quarter

To identify which campaign element to kill or fix first, email sequence decay is root-cause diagnosed before any recommendation — the analysis distinguishes between subject line fatigue (open rate dropping 38% to 22%) and body content failure (Email 3 click-to-open ratio collapsing to 1.4% CTR, poor tier vs 3.5-5% average) to prioritize the highest-leverage rewrite

To give the campaign manager an executable next-week plan, each recommendation specifies the exact change, expected metric lift, owner, and deadline — not 'improve the landing page' but a concrete intervention like reducing form fields or adding social proof with a projected conversion rate improvement from 6.9% toward the 10-20% average benchmark

To protect spend on what is already generating pipeline, a 'What is working — do not change' section explicitly names Email 1 CTR (5.2%, above the 3.5-5% average range), MQL-to-SAL rate (41%, within the 35-50% average benchmark), and budget pacing (50.7% spend at 50% duration) as assets to preserve from unnecessary optimization

To close the gap between 5 SQLs/week and the 4.4/week run rate needed for 35 SQLs, the webinar-to-opp rate (8%, poor tier vs 15-20% average) is diagnosed with root cause analysis exploring whether the problem is attendee qualification, post-webinar follow-up timing, or nurture sequence quality — because fixing this single metric could add 2-3 opps per webinar

To enable spend optimization, the landing page conversion rate (6.9%, below the 10-20% average benchmark) is connected to upstream spend efficiency — every percentage point improvement on the landing page reduces effective cost per lead across all traffic sources feeding it

evals.prompt:
I need a deep-dive on our LinkedIn channel. We're spending $11K/month and leadership is asking whether to cut it. Here's the last 4 weeks:

Week 1:
  Sponsored — Impressions: 38,200, Clicks: 210, CTR: 0.55%, Leads: 14, CPL: $196
  Thought Leader Ads — Impressions: 12,400, Clicks: 149, CTR: 1.2%, Leads: 8, CPL: $0 (organic boost)

Week 2:
  Sponsored — Impressions: 41,100, Clicks: 189, CTR: 0.46%, Leads: 11, CPL: $250
  Thought Leader Ads — Impressions: 11,800, Clicks: 118, CTR: 1.0%, Leads: 6, CPL: $0

Week 3:
  Sponsored — Impressions: 39,600, Clicks: 138, CTR: 0.35%, Leads: 7, CPL: $393
  Thought Leader Ads — Impressions: 9,200, Clicks: 74, CTR: 0.80%, Leads: 4, CPL: $0

Week 4:
  Sponsored — Impressions: 40,500, Clicks: 101, CTR: 0.25%, Leads: 4, CPL: $688
  Thought Leader Ads — Impressions: 8,100, Clicks: 52, CTR: 0.64%, Leads: 2, CPL: $0

Downstream from LinkedIn leads (all weeks combined):
  Total LinkedIn leads: 56
  MQL: 31 (55%)
  SAL: 18 (58% of MQL)
  Opp created: 9 (50% of SAL)
  Average deal size: $42K
  Pipeline generated: $378K
  Spend: $11,000
  Pipeline-to-spend ratio: 34:1

Our ICP is VP/Director of Ops at mid-market logistics companies (200-2000 employees).

Should we cut LinkedIn or not? What's actually going on here?
evals.expected_output: A structured channel deep-dive that refuses to recommend pausing LinkedIn without first diagnosing the creative/audience decay problem. The analysis should identify the clear week-over-week CTR decline trend (0.55% -> 0.46% -> 0.35% -> 0.25% for Sponsored; 1.2% -> 1.0% -> 0.80% -> 0.64% for Thought Leader), diagnose creative fatigue or audience saturation as root cause rather than channel failure, note that the pipeline-to-spend ratio of 34:1 is strong, highlight that the downstream conversion quality is healthy (55% lead-to-MQL, 58% MQL-to-SAL, 50% SAL-to-opp), recommend creative refresh and audience expansion before any budget cut, and explicitly name Thought Leader ad format and downstream conversion quality as things that are working.
expectations: To prevent a budget cut that would destroy $378K in pipeline from $11K in spend (34:1 ratio), the analysis explicitly refuses to recommend cutting LinkedIn without first testing creative and audience changes — directly applying the skill's NEVER rule about pausing channels before testing creative

To diagnose the real spend optimization opportunity, week-over-week CTR decline (Sponsored: 0.55% to 0.25%; Thought Leader: 1.2% to 0.64%) is identified as creative fatigue or audience saturation — a fixable execution problem, not a channel-level failure that warrants budget reallocation

To prove LinkedIn generates pipeline-ready leads worth protecting, downstream conversion quality (55% lead-to-MQL, 58% MQL-to-SAL, 50% SAL-to-opp) is benchmarked against the skill's reference table — MQL-to-SAL at 41% average benchmark makes 55% above-average, validating that the channel attracts ICP-fit prospects even as volume declines

To show leadership the difference between volume decline and ROI decline, pipeline-to-spend ratio (34:1) and average deal size ($42K) are positioned as the primary decision metrics — not impressions or CTR — reframing the cut-or-keep decision around revenue outcomes rather than vanity metrics

To give the marketing team an executable save-the-channel plan, specific creative refresh recommendations are provided — new ad variants to test, audience segment expansion beyond current targeting, or format mix shifts toward Thought Leader ads — with expected CTR recovery targets and a 2-week test timeline before any budget decision

To protect what is generating revenue, the 'What is working' section explicitly names Thought Leader ad format (higher CTR trajectory even while declining) and downstream lead quality as assets that a blanket budget cut would destroy alongside the underperforming Sponsored creative

To ensure the analysis follows the structured performance report format, the output must open with the mandatory output header (TASK, ICP MATCH, CONFIGURATION, VERIFY DATA) and use the 4-layer framework (ARE WE ON TRACK, WHY, WHAT TO DO, WHAT NOT TO CHANGE) with each layer clearly labeled — a general-purpose analysis would provide recommendations but not follow this specific structural template

evals.prompt:
We just finished a 2-week A/B test on our main lead gen landing page. Here are the results:

Variant A (Control — current page):
  Visitors: 1,247
  Form submissions: 112
  Conversion rate: 8.98%
  Avg time on page: 1:42
  Bounce rate: 61%
  Form field count: 7 (name, email, company, title, phone, company size, use case)
  Post-submission MQL rate: 34%

Variant B (Challenger — new design):
  Visitors: 1,253
  Form submissions: 198
  Conversion rate: 15.80%
  Avg time on page: 2:18
  Bounce rate: 44%
  Form field count: 4 (name, work email, company, title)
  Post-submission MQL rate: 19%

So Variant B gets way more conversions but the MQL rate dropped hard. The team is split on which to ship. We need 80 MQLs/month from this page to hit target.

Current monthly traffic to the page: ~2,600 visitors.

What's your analysis?
evals.expected_output: A structured A/B test analysis that goes beyond surface conversion rate to calculate effective MQL yield per visitor for each variant. Variant A: 8.98% x 34% = 3.05% effective MQL rate = ~79 MQLs/month at 2,600 visitors. Variant B: 15.80% x 19% = 3.00% effective MQL rate = ~78 MQLs/month. The analysis should diagnose that the conversion rate lift from reduced form fields is almost exactly offset by MQL quality drop, identify that neither variant reliably hits the 80 MQL target, recommend a Variant C that combines Variant B's design/UX improvements with a progressive profiling or enrichment strategy to recover lead quality, compare both variants against the landing page benchmark (average: 10-20%), and flag that Variant A's 8.98% is below average while Variant B's 15.80% is in the average-to-good range.
expectations: To reframe the team's decision around pipeline impact rather than conversion vanity metrics, effective MQL yield per visitor is calculated for both variants (A: 8.98% x 34% = 3.05% = ~79 MQLs/month; B: 15.80% x 19% = 3.00% = ~78 MQLs/month) — exposing that the raw conversion lift produces zero net gain in qualified pipeline

To tell the CMO whether this page can hit the 80 MQL/month target, both variants are assessed against the target and both are found insufficient — the analysis frames this as 'neither variant solves the business problem' rather than picking a winner from two losing options

To diagnose why the MQL rate dropped so the team can fix it, root cause analysis identifies that removing company size and use case fields eliminated the qualification signal — the form reduction increased volume but removed the data that separates qualified from unqualified leads

To generate a recommendation that actually hits the 80 MQL target, the analysis proposes a specific Variant C: Variant B's 4-field form with progressive profiling on the thank-you page or backend enrichment via Clearbit/ZoomInfo to recover qualification data without adding form friction — with projected MQL yield math

To benchmark both variants against industry standards, conversion rates are compared to the skill's landing page benchmark (average 10-20%) — Variant A (8.98%) classified as below average, Variant B (15.80%) classified as average-to-good — contextualizing the UX improvement as real even though MQL yield is flat

To protect the UX gains worth preserving regardless of which variant ships, the analysis identifies Variant B's lower bounce rate (44% vs 61%) and higher time-on-page (2:18 vs 1:42) as engagement improvements that indicate better page design — these should carry forward into any Variant C

To ensure the analysis follows the structured performance report format, the output must open with the mandatory output header (TASK, ICP MATCH, CONFIGURATION, VERIFY DATA) and benchmark both conversion rates against the skill's landing page reference table (average: 10-20%) with explicit tier classification — a general-purpose analysis might use benchmarks but would not apply the specific benchmark table from the skill
